title: Managing your files (browser & SFTP)
body:
Every server on ReFx Hosting gives you two ways to work with its files: a built-in file manager in your browser and full SFTP access for your favorite desktop client.

## The in-browser file manager

Open your server in the panel and go to the **Files** tab. This is the quickest way to make small changes without installing anything.

From here you can:

- **Browse** through folders to find configs, save data, and logs.
- **Edit** text files (like config or properties files) directly in the browser and save.
- **Upload** files by dragging them in or using the upload button.
- **Delete** files and folders you no longer need.

> Tip: Stop the server before editing important config files, then start it again so your changes are picked up cleanly.

## Connecting over SFTP

For bigger jobs — uploading lots of files, moving a whole world, or working with a desktop tool — SFTP is the way to go. We recommend a free client:

- **FileZilla** (Windows, macOS, Linux)
- **WinSCP** (Windows)

You'll find everything you need on the **Files** tab in the panel. Look for the SFTP details and copy them:

- **Host** — the SFTP address
- **Port** — the SFTP port
- **Username** — your SFTP username
- **Password** — your SFTP password

Then connect:

1. Open FileZilla or WinSCP.
2. Choose **SFTP** as the protocol (not plain FTP).
3. Enter the host, port, username, and password from the panel.
4. Connect, and your server's files will appear.
5. Drag files between your computer and the server to upload or download.

> Note: Always pick SFTP, not FTP. If your client asks about an unknown host key the first time, accepting it is normal.

## Tips for large uploads

Big uploads (modpacks, worlds, lots of assets) go more smoothly over SFTP than the browser:

- Use FileZilla or WinSCP rather than the browser file manager for anything large.
- If you have many files, zip them on your computer first, upload the single archive, then extract it on the server.
- Keep the server **stopped** while uploading world or save data so files aren't changed mid-transfer.
- If a transfer is interrupted, most SFTP clients let you resume or re-queue the failed files.

---

## Related articles

- [Finding and connecting to your server](/knowledge-base/finding-and-connecting-to-your-server)
- [Creating and restoring backups](/knowledge-base/creating-and-restoring-backups)
- [Using the live console](/knowledge-base/using-the-live-console)

Still stuck? Open a support ticket and our team will help.

title: Minecraft: getting started
body:
Spin up a Minecraft server, pick the right loader and version, make yourself an operator, and get your friends connected in minutes.

## Pick a loader and version

You choose your **loader** and **version** when you order, and you can change them anytime from the **Startup** tab in your server's control panel. The loader decides what kind of add-ons your server can run:

- **Vanilla** — the unmodified game, no plugins or mods.
- **Paper** — high-performance, vanilla-compatible, and supports **plugins**.
- **Fabric** — lightweight loader that supports **mods**.
- **Forge** — the classic mod loader, supports **mods**.
- **NeoForge** — a modern fork of Forge, supports **mods**.

Pick the loader your add-ons require, then select the Minecraft **version** you want to run. If you're not planning to mod, Vanilla or Paper is a great place to start.

> Tip: Want plugins or mods? See [Minecraft mods, plugins and modpacks](/knowledge-base/minecraft-mods-plugins-modpacks) for one-click modpack installs and how to add content.

After changing the loader or version, save your settings and **start** the server from the **Console** tab. The first start may take a moment.

## Basic server settings

Common options like the server name, difficulty, game mode, and max players can be set through your panel configuration fields or by editing the `server.properties` file on the **Files** tab. Use the in-browser file manager, or upload your own copy via SFTP — see [Managing files and SFTP](/knowledge-base/managing-files-and-sftp).

After editing `server.properties`, restart the server so the changes take effect.

## Make yourself an operator

Operators ("ops") have admin powers like changing the time, teleporting, and managing players. The easiest way to op yourself:

1. Start the server and join it once so the server knows your username.
2. Open the **Console** tab.
3. Type `op YourUsername` and send it.

You'll now have full admin commands in-game.

## Use a whitelist (optional)

A whitelist keeps your server private so only approved players can join. From the **Console** tab:

1. Send `whitelist on` to enable it.
2. Send `whitelist add Username` for each player you want to allow.
3. Send `whitelist reload` to apply your changes.

Remember to add yourself, too. If you prefer, you can manage ops and the whitelist directly through the **Files** tab instead.

## Connect to your server

Your server's connection address is shown in the panel. In Minecraft, choose **Multiplayer**, then **Add Server**, paste the address (with the port if one is shown), and join.

If you can't connect, confirm the server is running on the **Console** tab and see [Can't connect to your server](/knowledge-base/cant-connect-to-server).

---

Still stuck? Open a support ticket and our team will help.

title: Minecraft: mods, plugins & modpacks
body:
Add plugins, mods, or a full modpack to your Minecraft server — the right approach depends on which loader your server is running.

## First, know your loader

The type of add-on you can install depends on the loader you chose when you set up Minecraft:

- **Paper** runs **plugins** (server-side only — players don't need anything extra to join).
- **Fabric**, **Forge**, and **NeoForge** run **mods** (players usually need the matching mods installed too).
- **Vanilla** does not support plugins or mods.

You can view and change your loader and version on the **Startup** tab. If you need a different loader, switch first — see [Minecraft getting started](/knowledge-base/minecraft-getting-started).

> Tip: Always take a backup before installing or updating add-ons. If something breaks, you can restore in a couple of clicks. See [Creating and restoring backups](/knowledge-base/creating-and-restoring-backups).

## Install Paper plugins

1. Download the plugin `.jar` from the developer's site, making sure it matches your Minecraft version.
2. Stop the server on the **Console** tab.
3. Open the **Files** tab and go to the `plugins/` folder.
4. Upload the `.jar` into `plugins/` (drag-and-drop in the file manager, or use SFTP with a client like FileZilla or WinSCP).
5. Start the server and watch the console for the plugin loading without errors.

Most plugins create their own config file or folder inside `plugins/` on first start, which you can then edit in the **Files** tab.

## Install Fabric / Forge / NeoForge mods

1. Download each mod `.jar`, checking it is built for your **loader** and your **Minecraft version**.
2. Stop the server.
3. In the **Files** tab, open the `mods/` folder.
4. Upload every mod `.jar` into `mods/`.
5. Start the server and check the console for a clean startup.

Make sure every mod targets the same Minecraft and loader version — a single mismatched file is the most common cause of a server that won't start.

## One-click modpacks

The quickest route to a full modded experience is the one-click modpack install in the panel. Pick a modpack and the matching mods and configuration are set up for you, so you skip the manual file juggling.

After installing, players typically need that same modpack on their own client to join.

## Quick troubleshooting

- **Server won't start after a change?** Remove the last `.jar` you added and start again, then add mods back a few at a time.
- **Version mismatch** is the usual culprit — confirm the Minecraft version and loader match on every file.
- Still in trouble? Restore your pre-change backup and try again. See [Server won't start](/knowledge-base/server-wont-start).

---

For loaders, versions, and first-run basics, see [Minecraft getting started](/knowledge-base/minecraft-getting-started).

Still stuck? Open a support ticket and our team will help.

title: Palworld: getting started
body:
Your Palworld dedicated server is ready almost the moment payment clears — here's how to set a password, tune your world, and get everyone connected.

## Before you start

When you order a Palworld server with ReFx Hosting, it installs automatically (instant setup) and appears under **My Servers**. Open it to reach the server control panel.

If you haven't ordered yet, see [How to order a server](/knowledge-base/how-to-order-a-server). New to the panel? Try the [Panel tour](/knowledge-base/panel-tour) first.

## Set your passwords and world settings

Palworld's options — server name, server password, admin password, max players, and gameplay multipliers — are controlled by configuration fields in your panel. Depending on the product, you'll find these in:

- The **Startup** tab, as server variables you can view and edit.
- The **Files** tab, where you can open and edit the server settings in your browser.

A typical flow:

1. Open the **Console** tab and **Stop** the server if it's running.
2. Go to **Startup** (or **Files**) and set your **server name**, **server password** (so only people you share it with can join), and **admin password** (used for admin commands).
3. Adjust common world settings to taste — max players, day/night length, capture and breeding rates, EXP and gathering multipliers, and difficulty.
4. Save your changes.
5. Return to **Console** and **Start** the server.

> Tip: Change settings while the server is stopped, then start it. Edits made while it's running may not take effect until the next restart.

For more on what each variable does, see [Editing startup parameters](/knowledge-base/editing-startup-parameters).

## Connect to your server

Find your server's address and port on its panel page — see [Finding and connecting to your server](/knowledge-base/finding-and-connecting-to-your-server). In Palworld:

1. From the main menu choose **Join Multiplayer (Dedicated Server)**.
2. Enter the address and port shown in your panel.
3. If you set a server password, enter it when prompted.
4. Connect and start playing.

Share that same address, port, and password with friends so they can join.

## Managing your world

A few things that help once your server is live:

- **Backups:** Create one on demand or schedule automatic backups so a bad night never costs you your save. See [Creating and restoring backups](/knowledge-base/creating-and-restoring-backups).
- **Scheduled restarts:** Set a timed restart to keep things running smoothly via [Scheduling tasks](/knowledge-base/scheduling-tasks).
- **Helpers:** Add trusted people as [sub-users](/knowledge-base/sub-users-and-permissions) so they can manage the server without your main login.
- **More room:** Outgrowing your tier? The **Upgrade** tab lets you move up — see [Upgrading or downgrading your plan](/knowledge-base/upgrading-or-downgrading-your-plan).

If Palworld won't boot or players can't join, our [Server won't start](/knowledge-base/server-wont-start) and [Can't connect to server](/knowledge-base/cant-connect-to-server) guides cover the usual fixes.

---

Still stuck? Open a support ticket and our team will help.

title: A tour of the control panel
body:
Get to know your ReFx Hosting control panel — the dashboard navigation and the per-server tabs that let you run everything from one place.

When you log in, you land on your dashboard. The left-hand navigation stays the same everywhere, and clicking into any server opens a set of tabs for managing just that server. Here's the lay of the land.

## The left navigation

These links appear on every page:

- **Dashboard** — your home screen with an overview of your account and servers.
- **My Servers** — the list of everything you're running; click a server to open its control panel. See [Finding and connecting to your server](/knowledge-base/finding-and-connecting-to-your-server).
- **Order** — browse the catalog and buy a new server. See [How to order a server](/knowledge-base/how-to-order-a-server).
- **Billing** — invoices, payment methods, and account credit. See [Invoices, renewals and payment](/knowledge-base/invoices-renewals-and-payment).
- **Support** — open a ticket or browse the Knowledge Base.
- **Account** — change your password or email and set up two-factor security. See [Securing your account with 2FA](/knowledge-base/securing-your-account-2fa).

## The server control panel tabs

Open any server and you'll see tabs along the top. Which ones appear depends on the server type, but most game servers offer all of these:

- **Console** — live server output where you can Start, Stop, Restart, and send commands. See [Using the live console](/knowledge-base/using-the-live-console).
- **Files** — an in-browser file manager plus SFTP details for tools like FileZilla or WinSCP. See [Managing files and SFTP](/knowledge-base/managing-files-and-sftp).
- **Backups** — create backups on demand, schedule them, restore, and download. See [Creating and restoring backups](/knowledge-base/creating-and-restoring-backups).
- **Databases** — create a database and get connection credentials. See [Using databases](/knowledge-base/using-databases).
- **Schedules** — automate tasks on a cron-like timer, such as restarts or backups. See [Scheduling tasks](/knowledge-base/scheduling-tasks).
- **Sub-users** — invite people to help manage one server with fine-grained permissions, without sharing your login. See [Sub-users and permissions](/knowledge-base/sub-users-and-permissions).
- **Startup** — view and edit the startup command and server variables. See [Editing startup parameters](/knowledge-base/editing-startup-parameters).
- **Settings** — rename the server and adjust basic options. See [Renaming and server settings](/knowledge-base/renaming-and-server-settings).
- **Upgrade** — move to a higher or lower plan, hardware tier, or slot count. See [Upgrading or downgrading your plan](/knowledge-base/upgrading-or-downgrading-your-plan).

> Tip: Don't see a tab you expected? Some tabs only apply to certain server types — a TeamSpeak voice server or a web hosting plan won't show every option a game server does.

---

## Where to go next

If you're brand new, start with [Getting started with ReFx](/knowledge-base/getting-started-with-refx). Want to change which game your server runs without losing its address or backups? That's the [game switcher](/knowledge-base/switching-games-on-your-server).

Still stuck? Open a support ticket and our team will help.

title: Recovering your account (lost password or 2FA)
body:
Locked out of your ReFx Hosting account? Here's how to reset a forgotten password and what to do if you've lost your two-factor device.

## Reset a forgotten password

If you can't remember your password, you can reset it yourself by email:

1. On the sign-in page, choose the password reset option.
2. Enter the email address on your account.
3. Open the password reset email in your inbox and follow the link it contains.
4. Set a new password, then sign in with it.

> Tip: If the email doesn't arrive within a few minutes, check your spam folder and confirm you typed the same address you used to register. See [Verifying your email](/knowledge-base/verifying-your-email) if you're unsure which address is on file.

Once you're back in, you can also change your password (and email) any time under **Account**. See [Changing your password and email](/knowledge-base/changing-password-and-email).

## Lost your 2FA device

If you protected your account with two-factor authentication (2FA) and you've lost access to your authenticator app or passkey, you'll need our help to get back in.

For your security, only the account owner can regain access. Open a support request and our team will verify your identity before restoring access:

1. From a device where you can reach our site, go to the Support page or the help link on the sign-in screen.
2. Tell us you've lost your 2FA device and need to recover your account.
3. Provide the details our team asks for so we can confirm you own the account.

Once your identity is confirmed, we'll help you remove the lost 2FA method so you can sign in and set up a new one.

## Avoid getting locked out again

A few minutes of setup now saves a lot of stress later. We recommend:

- **Add a passkey.** A passkey (WebAuthn) can use your device biometrics or a security key, giving you a second way in that isn't tied to a single phone.
- **Use more than one method.** Having both an authenticator app (TOTP) and a passkey means losing one device won't lock you out.
- **Keep your email current.** Password resets go to your account email, so make sure it's an address you can always reach.

You can manage 2FA methods under **Account**. For full setup steps, see [Securing your account with 2FA](/knowledge-base/securing-your-account-2fa).

---

Still stuck? Open a support ticket and our team will help.

title: Reducing lag & improving performance
body:
If your server feels laggy, a few simple tweaks to mods, settings, restarts, location, and plan size usually clear it right up.

Lag almost always comes down to one thing: your server is being asked to do more than its current resources comfortably allow. Below are the most effective fixes, from quickest to most involved.

## Trim heavy mods and plugins

Mods and plugins are the number-one cause of slowdowns. Each one adds work, and a single poorly optimized one can drag everything down.

- Remove mods or plugins you no longer use.
- Add new ones a few at a time so you can spot the culprit if performance drops.
- Look for lightweight, performance-focused alternatives where they exist.

For Minecraft, see [Minecraft mods, plugins & modpacks](/knowledge-base/minecraft-mods-plugins-modpacks). For Steam-based games, see [Installing mods & Workshop content](/knowledge-base/installing-mods-and-workshop).

## Lower demanding in-game settings

Some settings cost a lot of performance for a small gain. Tune these in your game's configuration fields, the Files tab, or with in-game/console commands:

- Reduce **view distance** so the server tracks less of the world.
- Lower **entity** limits (mobs, animals, dropped items).
- Cap player **slots** to a realistic level for your group.

> **Tip:** Make one change at a time and watch the console after a restart so you know what actually helped.

## Schedule automatic restarts

A fresh server runs leaner than one that has been up for days, so a scheduled restart is one of the easiest wins.

1. Open the **Schedules** tab on your server.
2. Add a task to restart the server (for example, daily during quiet hours).
3. Save it and let it run automatically from then on.

See [Scheduling tasks](/knowledge-base/scheduling-tasks) for the full walkthrough.

## Pick a closer location

If players see high ping or rubber-banding rather than the server itself struggling, distance may be the issue. ReFx Hosting offers multiple regions, and a location nearer to most of your players reduces network lag.

You choose a location when you order, so for a big move the simplest path is a new server in a closer region. If most players connect fine and only the server is slow, location isn't the cause.

For connection-specific issues, see [Can't connect to your server](/knowledge-base/cant-connect-to-server).

## Upgrade your plan if you're maxed out

If you've trimmed mods and tuned settings but the server is consistently at its limit, it simply needs more horsepower. The **Upgrade** tab lets you move to a larger hardware tier or more slots, and an upgrade can apply immediately so you feel the difference right away.

- Check [Understanding server resources](/knowledge-base/understanding-server-resources) to see what your server is using.
- See [Upgrading or downgrading your plan](/knowledge-base/upgrading-or-downgrading-your-plan) for how the billing works.

---

Work through these in order — mods, settings, restarts, location, then an upgrade — and most lag clears up well before the last step.

Still stuck? Open a support ticket and our team will help.

title: Reinstalling your server
body:
Reinstalling your server reruns its setup from scratch to give you a clean install, and you can choose whether to keep or wipe your existing data.

## What a reinstall does

A reinstall runs your server's setup again, just like when it was first created. Your server keeps its identity throughout — the same address, SFTP details, backups, and subscription all stay exactly as they are. Only the game or product files are refreshed.

This is different from [switching games](/knowledge-base/switching-games-on-your-server), which changes your server to a different game entirely. A reinstall reinstalls the **same** game or product you already have.

## Keep or wipe your data

When you start a reinstall, you choose what happens to your existing files:

- **Keep your data** — your worlds, configs, mods, plugins, and uploads stay in place while the setup refreshes. Best when you just need to repair an install.
- **Wipe your data** — everything is cleared so you start fresh. Best when files are corrupted or you want a clean slate.

> Note: A wipe is permanent. If there's any chance you'll want your old files, [create a backup](/knowledge-base/creating-and-restoring-backups) first.

## When to use a reinstall

A reinstall is a good fix when:

- Your server **won't start** after a bad update, a broken mod, or an edited config you can't recover (see [Server won't start](/knowledge-base/server-wont-start)).
- Files have become **corrupted** and the server behaves strangely.
- You want a **fresh, clean install** without changing your plan or game.
- A guide tells you to reinstall to apply a change.

If your issue is just a crash or a misbehaving plugin, check the [live console](/knowledge-base/using-the-live-console) output first — sometimes you can fix it without a full reinstall.

## Back up first

Before reinstalling — even if you plan to keep your data — we strongly recommend taking a backup:

1. Open your server and go to the **Backups** tab.
2. Create a backup on demand and wait for it to finish.
3. Optionally **download** it to your own computer for safekeeping.

That way, if anything goes wrong, you can [restore](/knowledge-base/creating-and-restoring-backups) and be right back where you started.

## How to reinstall

1. Open the server you want to reinstall from **My Servers**.
2. From the **Console** tab, **stop** the server if it's running.
3. Take a backup from the **Backups** tab (recommended).
4. Start the reinstall from the panel and choose to **keep** or **wipe** your data.
5. Wait for the setup to complete — you can watch progress in the console.
6. **Start** the server again once it's ready.

Once it's back up, reconnect using the same address as before. If you need a refresher, see [Finding and connecting to your server](/knowledge-base/finding-and-connecting-to-your-server).

---

Still stuck? Open a support ticket and our team will help.

title: Renaming & basic server settings
body:
Renaming a server gives it a friendly label that's easy to recognize in your account, without changing the address anyone uses to connect.

## Where to find Settings

Open your server from **My Servers**, then click the **Settings** tab in the server control panel. This is where you rename your server and adjust the basic options ReFx Hosting exposes for it.

> Note: The exact options on the Settings tab can vary a little by server type. If you don't see something mentioned here, it isn't available for that product.

## Renaming your server

The server name is a **cosmetic label** only. It helps you tell servers apart in **My Servers** — it does not change how players, friends, or SFTP clients reach your server.

To rename a server:

1. Open the server and go to the **Settings** tab.
2. Type the new name in the server name field.
3. Save your changes.

The new name appears immediately in **My Servers** and across the panel.

> Tip: Use clear names like `Survival SMP` or `Rust Main` so you can spot the right server at a glance.

## The name does not change your address

This is the question people ask most, so to be clear, renaming your server:

- **Does not** change its connection address, IP, or port.
- **Does not** change your SFTP login details.
- **Does not** affect backups, databases, schedules, or your subscription.

Your connection address is shown in the panel and stays the same no matter what you call the server. To find it, see [Finding and connecting to your server](/knowledge-base/finding-and-connecting-to-your-server).

## What "basic settings" covers

The Settings tab is for small, account-level options. The bigger controls live on their own tabs:

- **In-game options** (the name shown in-game, passwords, slots, max players, RCON): set these in your game's configuration fields, the **Files** tab, or via in-game/console commands — not the Settings tab.
- **Startup command and variables**: use the **Startup** tab. See [Editing startup parameters](/knowledge-base/editing-startup-parameters).
- **Changing plan, tier, or slot count**: use the **Upgrade** tab. See [Upgrading or downgrading your plan](/knowledge-base/upgrading-or-downgrading-your-plan).
- **Swapping which game the server runs**: use the game switcher. See [Switching games on your server](/knowledge-base/switching-games-on-your-server).

---

## Quick FAQ

**Will renaming kick players off?** No. The name is just a label in your panel; live connections are unaffected.

**Can I rename more than once?** Yes, as often as you like.

**My friends can't connect after a rename — why?** Renaming never changes your address, so the cause is something else. Start with [Can't connect to your server](/knowledge-base/cant-connect-to-server).

Still stuck? Open a support ticket and our team will help.

title: Rust: getting started
body:
Your Rust server installs automatically and is ready to play in minutes — here's how to connect, configure it, run admin commands, schedule a wipe, and add mods.

## Connect to your server

Once your order is paid, Rust installs automatically and appears under **My Servers**. Open it and start the server from the **Console** tab.

To join in-game:

1. Find your server's address and port in the panel (see [Finding and connecting to your server](/knowledge-base/finding-and-connecting-to-your-server)).
2. In Rust, open the in-game console (press **F1**) and type `connect <address>:<port>`.
3. You can also find your server by name in the in-game server browser once it's online.

> Tip: Give the server a minute on first boot — Rust installs the latest game files automatically.

## Set the server name and other options

Your server name appears in the server browser. You can set this and other options (such as a password, slots, and admin settings) in a few ways:

- Use the **panel configuration fields** for your server, where available.
- Edit the server config in the **Files** tab (see [Managing files and SFTP](/knowledge-base/managing-files-and-sftp)).
- Set them with console commands such as `server.hostname` and `server.description`.

After changing config files, restart the server from the **Console** tab so changes take effect.

## Use RCON and the console

The **Console** tab gives you live server output and a command box — your RCON connection inside the panel. Start, stop, and restart the server there, and type Rust commands directly.

Common examples:

- `say <message>` — broadcast a message to players.
- `ownerid <steamID> "<name>" "<reason>"` — grant owner (admin) rights.
- `moderatorid <steamID> "<name>" "<reason>"` — grant moderator rights.
- `server.save` — force a save.

To make admin permissions stick across restarts, follow your owner/moderator command with `server.writecfg`.

## Schedule a wipe

A wipe resets the map and, optionally, player blueprints. To run one on a timer, use the **Schedules** tab to automate the task on a cron-like schedule.

1. Open the **Schedules** tab and create a new schedule.
2. Set the day and time you want the wipe to run.
3. Add the action — typically a restart paired with the command or steps your wipe needs.

For a full walkthrough, see [Scheduling tasks](/knowledge-base/scheduling-tasks). It's a good idea to [take a backup](/knowledge-base/creating-and-restoring-backups) before any wipe.

## Add mods

Where your plan supports it, Rust can run mods via a **Workshop tab or mod IDs** in the panel.

1. Stop the server from the **Console** tab.
2. Add the mods you want using the Workshop/mod fields in the panel.
3. Start the server again to apply them.

For step-by-step help, see [Installing mods and Workshop content](/knowledge-base/installing-mods-and-workshop). You can also upload or edit related files in the **Files** tab or over SFTP.

> Note: Keep your mods updated after major Rust updates to avoid errors.

---

Related: [Switching games on your server](/knowledge-base/switching-games-on-your-server) and [Upgrading or downgrading your plan](/knowledge-base/upgrading-or-downgrading-your-plan).

Still stuck? Open a support ticket and our team will help.

title: Scheduling automatic tasks
body:
Schedules let your server run tasks on its own — like a nightly restart, a recurring command, or an automatic backup — without you lifting a finger.

## What schedules can do

The **Schedules** tab in your server control panel runs tasks for you on a repeating, cron-like timetable. You can use it to:

- **Restart the server** on a timer (a nightly restart keeps things running smoothly).
- **Run a command** automatically (for example, a save or announcement command your game supports).
- **Take a backup** on a regular cadence so you always have a recent restore point.

Each schedule fires on the time pattern you set and keeps repeating until you pause or delete it.

## Create a schedule

1. Open your server and go to the **Schedules** tab.
2. Create a new schedule and give it a clear name (for example, `Nightly restart`).
3. Set the timing — minute, hour, day, and so on — so it runs when you want.
4. Add an action: restart, run a command, or take a backup.
5. Save it. The schedule is now active and runs on its next match.

You can add more than one action to a single schedule, and you can keep several schedules running side by side.

## Example: a nightly restart

A daily restart is one of the most popular schedules. To set one up:

1. Create a schedule named `Nightly restart`.
2. Set it to run once a day at a quiet time for your players — say, 4:00 AM in your region.
3. Add a **restart** action.
4. Save.

> Tip: Pick a low-traffic hour so a restart is barely noticed. If your game supports it, add a warning command before the restart so players get a heads-up.

## Example: automatic backups

You can take backups on a schedule instead of remembering to do them by hand:

1. Create a schedule (for example, `Daily backup`).
2. Set the timing — once a day is a common choice.
3. Add a **backup** action.
4. Save.

For more on restoring or downloading what you've saved, see [Creating and restoring backups](/knowledge-base/creating-and-restoring-backups).

## Tips for reliable schedules

- Give each schedule a descriptive name so you can tell them apart later.
- Stagger a restart and a backup so they don't run at the same minute.
- Commands run exactly as your game expects them — check your game's documentation for the right syntax.
- You can pause or delete a schedule anytime from the **Schedules** tab.

---

Schedules pair nicely with other panel tools — watch results in the [live console](/knowledge-base/using-the-live-console), and review your [backups](/knowledge-base/creating-and-restoring-backups) to confirm they're being created.

Still stuck? Open a support ticket and our team will help.

title: Securing your account (2FA & passkeys)
body:
Two-factor authentication (2FA) adds a second lock to your ReFx Hosting account, so even if someone learns your password they still can't get in.

## Why this matters

Your account controls real things: live game and voice servers, billing details, and saved payment methods. A password alone can be guessed, reused, or leaked from another site. A second factor means signing in needs something you *have* (your phone or a passkey) in addition to something you *know* (your password). It takes a couple of minutes and dramatically reduces the chance of an account takeover.

ReFx Hosting supports two kinds of second factor, and you can use either or both:

- **Authenticator app (TOTP)** — a 6-digit code that refreshes every 30 seconds.
- **Passkey (WebAuthn)** — a biometric (fingerprint or face), device PIN, or hardware security key.

## Set up an authenticator app (TOTP)

You'll need an authenticator app such as Google Authenticator, Authy, or 1Password.

1. Go to **Account** in the left navigation.
2. Find the two-factor authentication section and choose to add an authenticator app.
3. Scan the QR code with your app, or type in the setup key it shows.
4. Enter the 6-digit code from the app to confirm and finish.

From now on, you'll enter a fresh code from the app each time you sign in.

> Tip: Codes are time-based, so keep your phone's clock set to update automatically. If a code is rejected, wait for the next one and try again.

## Add a passkey (WebAuthn)

A passkey lets you sign in with your fingerprint, face, device PIN, or a security key — no code to type.

1. Go to **Account** and choose to add a passkey.
2. Follow your browser or device prompt (Touch ID, Windows Hello, Android, or your security key).
3. Confirm to save it to your account.

You can register more than one passkey — for example one on your laptop and one on your phone — so you're never tied to a single device.

## Keep backup access

The most important habit with 2FA is making sure you can still get in if a device is lost.

- **Register more than one factor.** Adding both a passkey and an authenticator app, or passkeys on two devices, gives you a fallback.
- **Don't keep your only second factor on a device you might wipe or lose.** Always have at least one method you can reach.
- **Changing phones?** Set up your authenticator app on the new device (or add a new passkey) *before* you retire the old one.

If you do get locked out, see [Recovering your account](/knowledge-base/recovering-your-account).

---

## Related security steps

- Change your password or email any time under **Account** — see [Changing your password and email](/knowledge-base/changing-password-and-email).
- For automation and scripts, use scoped, revocable [API keys](/knowledge-base/using-api-keys) instead of your login.
- New here? Start with the [panel tour](/knowledge-base/panel-tour).

Still stuck? Open a support ticket and our team will help.

title: My server won’t start
body:
When a server refuses to start, the console almost always tells you why — this checklist walks you through reading that message, undoing recent changes, and fixing the most common causes.

## Start with the console

The Console tab is your best friend here. When a server fails to start, it usually prints an error just before it stops.

1. Open your server and go to the **Console** tab.
2. Click **Start** and watch the live output.
3. Read the last several lines — look for words like `error`, `failed`, `missing`, `port`, or a version mismatch.

That message points you straight at the cause. Keep it handy — you'll want it if you contact support later.

> Tip: New to the console? See [Using the live console](/knowledge-base/using-the-live-console).

## Undo recent changes

If the server worked until you changed something, that change is the likely culprit.

- **Startup settings**: If you edited the startup command or a variable, revert it on the **Startup** tab. See [Editing startup parameters](/knowledge-base/editing-startup-parameters).
- **Config files**: A typo in a config file you edited (Files tab or SFTP) can stop a server cold. Restore or correct it.
- **Mods or plugins**: A broken or incompatible add-on is one of the most common reasons a server won't boot. Remove the last one you added and try again. See [Installing mods and Workshop](/knowledge-base/installing-mods-and-workshop).

## Confirm the right version and loader

Mismatched versions are a frequent cause, especially after an update.

- **Minecraft**: Make sure your loader (Vanilla, Paper, Fabric, Forge, NeoForge) and version match your plugins or mods. An add-on built for one version or loader won't load on another. See [Minecraft mods, plugins & modpacks](/knowledge-base/minecraft-mods-plugins-modpacks).
- **Other games**: Confirm the game version in your settings matches what your save, mods, or players expect.

## Check your resources

If the console mentions running out of memory, or the server starts then stops soon after, you may have outgrown your current plan.

- Heavy modpacks and high player counts need more RAM.
- You can move to a larger tier or slot count anytime on the **Upgrade** tab.

See [Understanding server resources](/knowledge-base/understanding-server-resources) and [Upgrading or downgrading your plan](/knowledge-base/upgrading-or-downgrading-your-plan).

## Try a reinstall (back up first)

If your files got into a bad state, a reinstall gives you a clean base.

1. Go to the **Backups** tab and create a backup so your world and data are safe.
2. Reinstall your server (see [Reinstalling your server](/knowledge-base/reinstalling-your-server)).
3. Start it again and watch the console.

> Note: Always back up before reinstalling. See [Creating and restoring backups](/knowledge-base/creating-and-restoring-backups).

---

## A few quick checks

- **TeamSpeak**: The server won't run until you accept the TeamSpeak license — toggle it on the **Voice** tab. See [TeamSpeak getting started](/knowledge-base/teamspeak-getting-started).
- **Billing**: A suspended server won't start. Check the **Billing** tab for unpaid invoices. See [Invoices, renewals & payment](/knowledge-base/invoices-renewals-and-payment).

If you're still stuck, copy the last lines of your console output and open a ticket with them included — that lets our team pinpoint the problem fast. See also [Can't connect to your server](/knowledge-base/cant-connect-to-server).

Still stuck? Open a support ticket and our team will help.

title: Adding sub-users & permissions
body:
Sub-users let you invite trusted helpers to manage a single server with exactly the permissions you choose, so you never have to share your main login.

## Why use sub-users?

Maybe a friend co-runs your Minecraft world, or a moderator needs console access but nothing else. Instead of handing over your account, you add them as a **sub-user** on just that one server. Each helper signs in with their own ReFx Hosting account, and you decide precisely what they can see and do.

- **No shared password** — your account, billing, and other servers stay private.
- **Per-server only** — a sub-user can reach the one server you invited them to, and nothing else.
- **Fine-grained control** — grant only the tabs and actions you want them to use.

## Adding a sub-user

1. Open the server from **My Servers** and go to the **Sub-users** tab.
2. Choose to invite a new sub-user and enter their email address.
3. Tick the permissions you want them to have (see below).
4. Save the invite. They'll get access once they sign in to their own account.

> Note: Your helper needs their own ReFx Hosting account. If they don't have one yet, they can create one and verify their email first — see [Getting started with ReFx](/knowledge-base/getting-started-with-refx).

## Choosing permissions

Permissions are grouped by what each panel tab does, so you can mix and match. Common examples include:

- **Console** — view live output, start/stop/restart, and send commands.
- **Files** — browse, edit, upload, and delete files (and use SFTP).
- **Backups** — create, restore, and download backups.
- **Databases** — create databases and view connection credentials.
- **Schedules** — set up automated tasks.
- **Startup** — view or edit the startup command and variables.
- **Settings** — rename the server and change basic settings.

Give people the minimum they need. A moderator might only get **Console**, while a co-admin building your world might also get **Files** and **Backups**. You stay the owner and keep full control.

> Tip: Not sure what each tab does? The [panel tour](/knowledge-base/panel-tour) walks through every server tab.

## Removing or editing a sub-user

You can change a helper's access at any time, or remove them entirely:

1. Go to the **Sub-users** tab on the server.
2. Find the person in the list.
3. Edit their permissions and save, or remove them to revoke all access immediately.

Once removed, that person can no longer reach the server. Their own account and any other servers they own are unaffected.

---

A few good habits:

- Review your sub-users now and then and remove anyone who no longer needs access.
- Encourage helpers to secure their own accounts with two-factor authentication — see [Securing your account (2FA)](/knowledge-base/securing-your-account-2fa).
- For unattended tools or scripts, use a scoped [API key](/knowledge-base/using-api-keys) instead of a sub-user.

Still stuck? Open a support ticket and our team will help.

title: Switching the game on your server
body:
Game switching lets you change which game your server runs while keeping the same server identity — its address, SFTP details, backups, and subscription all stay put.

## Why switching is so handy

With most hosts, trying a new game means ordering a whole new server. At ReFx Hosting, your server keeps its identity and you simply swap the game it runs. That means:

- The same connection address and SFTP details carry over.
- Your existing backups stay attached to the server.
- Your subscription and billing interval are unchanged — no new order.

It's the fastest way to bounce between, say, Minecraft this week and Palworld the next.

## Before you switch

A few quick things to know first:

- **Available games depend on your plan.** The game switcher only shows games your plan can run. If a game you want isn't listed, you may need to upgrade — see [Upgrading or downgrading your plan](/knowledge-base/upgrading-or-downgrading-your-plan).
- **Take a backup first.** Even if you plan to keep your data, a fresh backup is cheap insurance. See [Creating and restoring backups](/knowledge-base/creating-and-restoring-backups).
- **Decide: keep or wipe.** During the switch you'll choose whether to keep your existing files or start clean. Since games can't usually read another game's files, a wipe often gives the cleanest result.

> Tip: Keeping your data is great when you switch and later switch back — your old world or files will still be there.

## How to switch your game

1. Open the server from **My Servers** in the dashboard.
2. Go to the **Console** tab and **Stop** the server. It must be fully stopped before you can switch.
3. Open the game switcher in the server's control panel.
4. Pick the new game you'd like to run from the available list.
5. Choose to **keep** your existing data or **wipe** it and start fresh.
6. Confirm. The new game installs automatically.

Once setup finishes, head back to the **Console** tab and **Start** the server. Your new game is ready on the same address as before — see [Finding and connecting to your server](/knowledge-base/finding-and-connecting-to-your-server).

## After the switch

A few things may differ on the new game:

- **Startup options and variables** are specific to each game. Check the **Startup** tab to review the startup command and any server variables. See [Editing startup parameters](/knowledge-base/editing-startup-parameters).
- **In-game settings** like server name, password, slots, and admin/RCON are configured per game via the panel's configuration fields, the **Files** tab, or in-game commands.
- If you switched to **Minecraft**, you can pick a loader and version and add plugins or mods — see [Minecraft getting started](/knowledge-base/minecraft-getting-started).

If you wiped your data and want your old setup back, switch back to the original game and restore from a backup you took beforehand.

---

Still stuck? Open a support ticket and our team will help.

title: TeamSpeak: becoming admin & managing your server
body:
Claiming server admin on your TeamSpeak server takes about a minute, and from there you can build channels, set permissions, and manage who can do what.

Before you start, make sure your server is running. If you just ordered it, accept the TeamSpeak license toggle on the **Voice** tab first — your server will not start until you do. See [TeamSpeak: getting started](/knowledge-base/teamspeak-getting-started) if you have not connected yet.

## Claim server admin with the privilege key

When a new TeamSpeak server is created, nobody is admin yet. You claim admin by entering a one-time **privilege key** (sometimes called a token) the first time you connect. ReFx Hosting shows this key on your server's **Voice** tab in the panel.

1. Connect to your server using the voice address and port shown in the panel.
2. Copy the **privilege key** from the **Voice** tab.
3. In your TeamSpeak client, open the option to use a privilege key, paste the key, and confirm.
4. You are now **Server Admin** and will see an admin badge next to your name.

> Tip: A privilege key works only once. After you claim admin it is used up, so keep it safe and do not share it — anyone with the key could grab admin instead of you.

## ServerQuery admin login

Your **Voice** tab also shows a **ServerQuery** admin login. ServerQuery is the administrative account for your TeamSpeak server, useful for advanced management. Treat these credentials like a password and keep them private.

---

## Create and arrange channels

Once you are admin, you can shape your server from the TeamSpeak client:

- Create new channels under your server.
- Give each channel a name, an optional password, and choose whether it is temporary or permanent.
- Reorder channels or group them to keep a long list tidy.

## Set permissions and manage users

TeamSpeak uses **server groups** (such as Admin, Normal, and Guest) and **channel groups** to control what people can do.

- Assign a user to a server or channel group to promote or demote them.
- Adjust what each group is allowed to do, such as talking, moving users, kicking, banning, or editing channels.
- To give a friend admin, generate another privilege key for them or add them to your admin group directly.
- Kick, ban, or move users as needed.

> Note: Take your time with group permissions. Granting a powerful group to the wrong person hands them broad control of your server.

If you would rather not give out full TeamSpeak admin but still want help managing the server inside ReFx Hosting, you can invite [sub-users with fine-grained permissions](/knowledge-base/sub-users-and-permissions) to your panel without sharing your main login.

---

Still stuck? Open a support ticket and our team will help.

title: TeamSpeak: getting started
body:
Setting up a TeamSpeak voice server on ReFx Hosting takes just a few minutes: order a plan, accept the license, and connect with the TeamSpeak client.

## Choosing a plan

TeamSpeak servers use flat monthly plans sized by **slot capacity** — the number of people who can be connected at the same time. Pick a slot count that comfortably fits your group, with a little room to grow.

When you're ready, open **Order** and switch to the **Voice Servers** tab. From there:

1. Choose your TeamSpeak plan and slot capacity.
2. Pick a server location close to your members for the lowest latency.
3. Name your server and continue to checkout.
4. Pay by card or PayPal — you can also apply a coupon, redeem a gift card, or use account credit.

Your voice server provisions automatically and appears under **My Servers**. New to ordering? See [How to order a server](/knowledge-base/how-to-order-a-server).

> Note: You'll need to verify your email before you can order. See [Verifying your email](/knowledge-base/verifying-your-email).

---

## Accepting the TeamSpeak license

Before your server can run, TeamSpeak requires you to accept its license **one time**. In your server's control panel, open the **Voice** tab and flip the license toggle to accept.

This is a quick, one-off step — once it's done, your server starts up and stays ready for you.

## Finding your voice address and port

Open your server and look at the **Voice** tab. There you'll find the **voice address and port** your members use to connect. Keep this handy — it's all anyone needs to join.

## Connecting with the TeamSpeak client

1. Download and install the TeamSpeak client on your computer.
2. Open it and choose to connect to a server.
3. Enter the **address and port** shown in your panel.
4. Pick a nickname and connect.

Share that same address and port with your friends so they can join too.

## Claiming server admin

The first time you connect, you'll want to become the server's admin so you can create channels and set permissions. Your panel's **Voice** tab shows the **ServerQuery admin login** and **privilege key** used to claim admin rights.

When your client prompts for a privilege key, enter the key from your panel to grant yourself full admin. After that you can build out channels and fine-tune permissions however you like.

For a deeper walkthrough of channels and permissions, see [TeamSpeak admin and permissions](/knowledge-base/teamspeak-admin-and-permissions).

---

## Need more (or fewer) slots?

If your community grows, you can move to a larger plan at any time from the **Upgrade** tab — see [Upgrading or downgrading your plan](/knowledge-base/upgrading-or-downgrading-your-plan). Your server renews automatically each month, and you'll find invoices under **Billing**.

> Tip: If members can't connect, double-check they're using the exact address and port from your panel. [Can't connect to server](/knowledge-base/cant-connect-to-server) has more fixes.

Still stuck? Open a support ticket and our team will help.

title: Understanding CPU, RAM & disk
body:
Every server you rent from ReFx Hosting runs on three core resources — CPU, RAM, and disk — and knowing what each one does makes it easy to pick the right tier and spot when it's time to upgrade.

## The three resources at a glance

- **CPU (processing power)**: How fast your server can do work. Game worlds, player actions, physics, and mods all use CPU. More CPU generally means smoother performance and less lag, especially with many players or heavy mods.
- **RAM (memory)**: Your server's short-term workspace. Bigger worlds, more players, and more plugins or mods all need more RAM. Run low and the server can crash, fail to start, or stutter.
- **Disk (storage)**: Where your world files, backups, mods, and uploads live. Web hosting plans also use disk for your website files. ReFx Hosting uses fast NVMe storage, so loads and saves stay quick.

## How tiers map to your usage

When you order, you choose a hardware tier (sized small to large) or, for some products, a slot count. Larger tiers bundle more CPU, RAM, and disk together.

A rough way to think about it:

- **Small tiers** suit a handful of friends, vanilla or lightly modded play, smaller voice rooms, and Starter web hosting.
- **Medium tiers** fit growing communities, modest modpacks, and busier servers.
- **Large tiers** handle big player counts, heavy modpacks, and demanding games where every bit of CPU and RAM counts.

Exact player counts vary by game and by how heavily it's modded — a vanilla Minecraft world supports far more players per gigabyte than a large modpack. Use the tier sizes in the catalog as your guide, and start one step up if you plan to add mods.

> Tip: Voice servers are sized by slot count rather than a hardware tier — just pick the number of slots you need. See [TeamSpeak getting started](/knowledge-base/teamspeak-getting-started).

## Signs you should upgrade

Watch for these during normal play:

- Lag, rubber-banding, or rising ping when several players are online.
- The server crashing, restarting on its own, or failing to start after you add mods or plugins.
- "Out of memory" messages, or worlds and backups failing to save.
- Running low on disk space for new worlds, uploads, or backups.

If you see these, your server has likely outgrown its current resources. For ways to squeeze out more performance first, read [Reducing lag and performance](/knowledge-base/reducing-lag-and-performance).

## Upgrading is easy

You don't lose anything by moving up — your server keeps its address, files, and backups. Just use the **Upgrade** tab in the panel.

1. Open the server, then go to the **Upgrade** tab.
2. Choose the higher plan, tier, or slot count.
3. Pay the prorated invoice to apply the change immediately.

Mid-cycle upgrades raise a prorated invoice you pay to apply right away; downgrades take effect at your next renewal. See [Upgrading or downgrading your plan](/knowledge-base/upgrading-or-downgrading-your-plan) and [Invoices, renewals & payment](/knowledge-base/invoices-renewals-and-payment) for the billing details.

---

Still stuck? Open a support ticket and our team will help.

title: Upgrading or downgrading your plan
body:
Need more power or want to scale back? You can change your plan, hardware tier, or slot count at any time from your server's Upgrade tab.

## How to change your plan

1. Go to **My Servers** and open the server you want to change.
2. Click the **Upgrade** tab.
3. Choose a higher or lower plan, a different hardware tier (small to large), or a new slot count, depending on what your product supports.
4. Review the change and confirm.

The panel walks you through the rest, including any invoice you need to pay.

## Upgrades apply once you pay

When you move up to a bigger tier or add slots mid-cycle, ReFx Hosting raises a **prorated invoice**. Proration means you only pay the difference between your old and new plan for the time left in your current billing period — you're never charged again for time you already paid for.

Pay that invoice and your upgrade applies **immediately**, so you get the extra resources right away.

> **Tip:** You can settle the prorated invoice with card, PayPal, or any account credit you have on file. See [Invoices, renewals, and payment](/knowledge-base/invoices-renewals-and-payment).

## Downgrades apply at the next renewal

Moving to a smaller plan or fewer slots works a little differently. To avoid losing time you've already paid for, **downgrades take effect at your next renewal** rather than right away.

Your server keeps running on its current plan until then, and your new lower rate kicks in automatically on the renewal date.

> **Note:** Changing your plan never changes your server's identity. Your address, SFTP details, backups, and existing data all stay exactly as they are.

## What changing your plan affects

- **More resources** — higher tiers give you more CPU, RAM, and disk for better performance.
- **More slots** — slot-based products let you raise or lower your player or user capacity.
- **Your billing amount** — your recurring invoice updates to match the new plan.

If you're not sure which tier you need, see [Understanding server resources](/knowledge-base/understanding-server-resources) and [Reducing lag and performance](/knowledge-base/reducing-lag-and-performance).

---

Still stuck? Open a support ticket and our team will help.

title: Using API keys
body:
API keys let you connect your own scripts and tools to your ReFx Hosting account so you can automate tasks instead of clicking through the panel by hand.

## What an API key is for

An API key is a secret token that proves a request is coming from you, without you having to log in each time. If you build automation, run your own dashboards, or use third-party tools that work with ReFx Hosting, an API key is how they sign in on your behalf.

Keys are **scoped**, meaning each key can be limited to only what it needs to do. That way a script that just checks server status doesn't also have permission to make bigger changes. If you don't run any automation, you don't need an API key at all.

## Creating a key

1. Go to **Account** in the left navigation.
2. Open the **API keys** section.
3. Create a new key and give it a clear name (for example, "backup script" or "status monitor") so you can recognize it later.
4. Choose the scope — keep it as limited as possible for what the key needs to do.
5. Save, then copy the key value.

> **Important:** The full key value is typically shown only once, at creation time. Copy it somewhere safe right away. If you lose it, you can't view it again and will need to create a new one.

## Keeping keys secret

Treat an API key like a password. Anyone who has it can act on your account within that key's scope.

- Don't paste keys into chat, tickets, screenshots, or public code.
- Don't commit keys to a public code repository.
- Give each tool or script its own key, so you can revoke one without breaking the others.
- Use the narrowest scope that still gets the job done.

## Revoking a key

If a key is no longer needed, or you think it may have leaked, revoke it. From the **API keys** section in **Account**, delete or revoke the key. It stops working right away, and anything using it will need a new key.

It's good practice to remove keys you no longer use, even if nothing has gone wrong — fewer active keys means less to keep safe.

---

For more ways to lock down your account, see [Securing your account with 2FA](/knowledge-base/securing-your-account-2fa) and [Changing your password and email](/knowledge-base/changing-password-and-email).

Still stuck? Open a support ticket and our team will help.

title: Using databases
body:
Many games, plugins, and web apps need a database to store data, and you can create one for your server right in the ReFx Hosting panel.

## When you need a database

Some software keeps its data in a database rather than in plain files. Common examples are Minecraft plugins (player stats, economies, login systems) and web apps. If a plugin or app asks for database connection details, this is where you get them.

If your server doesn't need a database, you can skip this. It's an optional extra you only set up when something asks for it.

## Create a database

1. Open the server's control panel from **My Servers**.
2. Go to the **Databases** tab.
3. Choose to create a new database and confirm.
4. The panel creates it and shows the connection credentials.

That's it — the database is ready to use right away.

## Find your connection credentials

After you create a database, the **Databases** tab shows everything you need to connect:

- **Host** — the address of the database.
- **Port** — the port to connect on.
- **Database name** — the name of the database you created.
- **Username** — the account used to connect.
- **Password** — the password for that account.

Keep these details private, just like any login. Anyone who has them can read and change your data.

> Tip: Copy the credentials straight from the panel rather than typing them by hand — small typos in the host or password are the most common reason a connection fails.

## Connect something to it

How you enter these details depends on what you're connecting:

- **A Minecraft plugin or mod**: open the plugin's config file in the **Files** tab and paste the host, port, database name, username, and password into its database section. Save, then restart the server so the plugin picks up the change. See [Managing files and SFTP](/knowledge-base/managing-files-and-sftp).
- **A web app**: put the same values into your app's configuration, such as its install screen or its config file in the `public/` folder. See [Web hosting: getting started](/knowledge-base/web-hosting-getting-started).
- **A database tool on your computer**: enter the host, port, username, password, and database name into your client to browse the data directly.

Every tool labels these fields a little differently, but they all map to the same five values shown in the panel.

---

## Troubleshooting

- **Connection refused or timed out**: check that the host and port match the panel exactly.
- **Access denied**: re-copy the username and password — a stray space is easy to miss.
- **Plugin still can't connect after editing the config**: make sure you saved the file and restarted the server.

For more on restarting and reading errors, see [Using the live console](/knowledge-base/using-the-live-console).

Still stuck? Open a support ticket and our team will help.

title: Using the live console
body:
The Console tab is your server's command center — start, stop, and restart your server, watch its live output, and type commands straight to it.

## Opening the console

Go to **My Servers**, click the server you want, and open the **Console** tab. It's usually the first thing you see when you open a server. New to the layout? See the [panel tour](/knowledge-base/panel-tour).

## Start, Stop, and Restart

At the top of the console you'll find the power controls:

- **Start** — boots your server. The first start after a fresh setup or game switch can take a little longer while files finish installing.
- **Stop** — shuts the server down so players disconnect cleanly.
- **Restart** — stops and starts in one click. Handy after changing settings or installing a mod.

> Tip: Use Stop or Restart from the console rather than just closing the tab. Closing your browser doesn't turn the server off — it keeps running until you stop it.

## Reading the live output

The large text area is your live log. It streams everything your server prints in real time — startup messages, player joins and leaves, errors, and warnings.

- Scroll up to read back through recent output.
- When something goes wrong, look here first. The last few lines before a crash usually point to the cause.
- A "running" status near the controls confirms your server is online.

If your server starts and then stops on its own, the output will usually tell you why — see [server won't start](/knowledge-base/server-wont-start).

## Sending commands

Below the log is a command input box. Type a command, press Enter, and it's sent straight to your running server. Any response appears in the live output above.

A few examples of what you might send:

1. Save your world or flush data before a restart.
2. Grant a player admin or operator rights.
3. Broadcast a message to everyone online.
4. Kick or ban a disruptive player.

The exact commands depend on the game you're running. For game-specific examples, check that game's article, such as [Minecraft getting started](/knowledge-base/minecraft-getting-started) or [Rust getting started](/knowledge-base/rust-getting-started).

### Console and RCON commands

Some games accept console or RCON-style commands directly in this box, so you can manage your server live without an in-game admin login. If a game uses RCON, you'll usually set or enable its password in the panel's configuration fields or the [startup parameters](/knowledge-base/editing-startup-parameters) first.

> Note: The server must be running for commands to work. If the input is greyed out or commands do nothing, press **Start** and wait for the log to show the server is fully online.

---

The console only sends commands to a live server. To change files, edit configs, or set the startup command, use the [Files tab](/knowledge-base/managing-files-and-sftp) and [Startup tab](/knowledge-base/editing-startup-parameters) instead.

Still stuck? Open a support ticket and our team will help.

title: Valheim: getting started
body:
Your Valheim server on ReFx Hosting installs automatically and is ready in minutes — this guide covers naming your world, setting a password, connecting, and adding mods.

## Set your world name and password

When your server provisions, it appears under **My Servers**. Open it to reach the control panel, then set up your world before you start it.

1. Go to the **Startup** tab to view and edit your server variables.
2. Set the **world name** (the save your server loads or creates) and a **server password** in the configuration fields.
3. Save your changes.
4. Open the **Console** tab and press **Start**.

> Tip: Pick a password you're comfortable sharing, and give it only to people you want on your world.

You can adjust the public server name and other options in the **Startup** tab. If a setting isn't shown as a field, you can edit the relevant config file in the **Files** tab.

## Connect to your server

Once the console shows the server is up and running, you have two ways in:

- **Server browser**: In Valheim, choose **Join Game**, switch to the **Community** list, search for your public server name, select it, and enter your password.
- **Direct connect**: Use the **Join IP** option and enter the address and port shown in your panel. This is usually the fastest, most reliable way to connect.

You'll find the exact address and port on your server's overview in the panel. For more, see [Finding and connecting to your server](/knowledge-base/finding-and-connecting-to-your-server).

> Note: It can take a minute after pressing Start for the server to appear in the community browser. Direct connect usually works sooner.

## Add mods

You can add mods to your Valheim server through the panel:

1. Stop the server from the **Console** tab.
2. Open the **Files** tab to use the in-browser file manager, or connect over **SFTP** with a client like FileZilla or WinSCP — the host, port, username, and password are all shown in the panel.
3. Upload your mod files, following the instructions provided with each mod.
4. Start the server again and check the **Console** for any load or error messages.

Keep these points in mind:

- Players generally need the **same mods and versions** installed locally to join a modded server.
- Update your mods when Valheim updates to avoid load errors.
- Take a backup before any big mod change.

For managing files and credentials, see [Managing files and SFTP](/knowledge-base/managing-files-and-sftp). For a broader overview of mods across games, see [Installing mods and Workshop content](/knowledge-base/installing-mods-and-workshop).

---

## Before you experiment

Always create a backup before installing mods or changing major settings — make one on demand or schedule automatic backups from the **Backups** tab. See [Creating and restoring backups](/knowledge-base/creating-and-restoring-backups).

If your server won't launch after a change, see [Server won't start](/knowledge-base/server-wont-start). If you can't connect, see [Can't connect to your server](/knowledge-base/cant-connect-to-server).

Still stuck? Open a support ticket and our team will help.

title: Verifying your email address
body:
Verifying your email is a quick one-time step that confirms your account before you can place your first order.

## Why verification is required

When you create your ReFx Hosting account, we send a verification link to the email address you signed up with. Confirming it keeps your account secure, makes sure we can reach you about your servers and billing, and unlocks ordering.

You must verify your email **before you can place an order**. Until then, checkout stays locked. Once verified, you can browse Game Servers, Voice Servers, and Web Hosting and check out as normal.

## How to verify

1. Open the inbox for the address you used to sign up.
2. Find the message from ReFx Hosting with your verification link.
3. Click the link in that email.
4. You're returned to your account, ready to order.

That's it — verification sticks, so you won't need to repeat it.

## Resending the link

Didn't get the email, or the link expired? Request a fresh one:

- Sign in and look for the verification prompt or banner.
- Choose the option to resend the verification email.
- Wait a minute or two, then check your inbox again.

A new link replaces any older one, so always use the most recent email you received.

## If it doesn't arrive

Verification emails are usually instant. If yours is missing, work through these checks:

1. **Check spam and junk.** Automated emails sometimes land in spam, promotions, or other filtered folders. If you find it there, mark it as "not spam" so future messages reach your inbox.
2. **Confirm the address is correct.** A small typo when signing up means the email went somewhere you can't see. Double-check the spelling on your account.
3. **Give it a few minutes.** Occasional delays happen — wait a short while before resending.
4. **Resend the link** using the steps above if it still hasn't shown up.

> Tip: If your email address has a typo, update it under **Account** once you're signed in, then resend the verification link to the corrected address. See [Changing your password and email](/knowledge-base/changing-password-and-email).

---

Once your email is verified, you're ready to rent your first server. Head over to [How to order a server](/knowledge-base/how-to-order-a-server), or start with [Getting started with ReFx](/knowledge-base/getting-started-with-refx) for a full walkthrough.

Still stuck? Open a support ticket and our team will help.

title: Web hosting: getting started
body:
Web hosting on ReFx Hosting lets you put a website online in minutes by uploading your files to the `public/` folder and viewing it at the address shown in your panel.

## Before you start

Once your web hosting plan is ordered, it provisions instantly and appears under **My Servers**. Open it to reach the control panel, where you will find your file manager, your SFTP details, and the live address for your site.

If you have not ordered yet, see [How to order a server](/knowledge-base/how-to-order-a-server). New to the panel? Take the [panel tour](/knowledge-base/panel-tour) first.

## Upload your website files

Your site is served from the `public/` folder. Anything you place there (HTML, CSS, JS, images) becomes part of your live website.

You have two ways to upload, and you can mix and match:

- **File manager** (in the browser): open the **Files** tab, go into `public/`, and use **Upload** to add files. You can also create, edit, and delete files right in the panel.
- **SFTP** (handy for larger sites): the **Files** tab shows your SFTP **host, port, username, and password**. Plug these into a client like **FileZilla** or **WinSCP**, connect, then drop your files into `public/`.

Full details on both methods are in [Managing files and SFTP](/knowledge-base/managing-files-and-sftp).

> Tip: Always upload into `public/`. Files left outside that folder will not appear on your live site.

## View your site

Once your files are in `public/`, open the address shown for your server in any web browser. Edit a file or upload a new one, refresh the page, and your changes appear.

## Going live with your own domain

Your site starts on the default address ReFx Hosting gives you, which is perfect for testing. When you are ready to use your own domain name:

1. Add your custom domain in the panel.
2. Point its DNS (an A or AAAA record) at the IP shown.
3. SSL is then issued and renewed automatically, so your site loads over `https://`.

Step-by-step instructions are in [Connecting a custom domain](/knowledge-base/connecting-a-custom-domain).

## Room to grow

Web hosting plans range from **Starter** to **Pro**, with more CPU, RAM, and disk as your site gets busier. If you outgrow your current plan, you can move up (or down) at any time from the **Upgrade** tab.

- Compare plans in [Choosing a web hosting plan](/knowledge-base/choosing-a-web-hosting-plan).
- Change plans with [Upgrading or downgrading your plan](/knowledge-base/upgrading-or-downgrading-your-plan).

---

Still stuck? Open a support ticket and our team will help.

title: Minecraft: Simple Voice Chat setup (proximity voice)
body:
Simple Voice Chat adds proximity voice to your Minecraft server. It needs one **UDP port** open — and the key thing to know is that on ReFx Hosting your server is given **one port**, published on both TCP *and* UDP. Voice chat has to use that port (or another port we allocate to you), not its built-in default of 24454.

## Why the default doesn't work

Out of the box, Simple Voice Chat starts its own UDP listener on port **24454**. Your server was never allocated that port, so nothing outside can reach it and voice fails to connect. You have to point voice chat at a port that *is* published for your server.

## Option A — share your server's port (easiest)

Tell voice chat to reuse your Minecraft server's port. Because we publish that port on UDP as well as TCP, voice then works with no extra setup.

1. Open **Files** in your panel and edit `voicechat/voicechat-server.properties` (it appears after the server has started once — Paper/plugin users: `plugins/voicechat/voicechat-server.properties`).
2. Set:
   ```
   port=-1
   bind_address=0.0.0.0
   ```
   `port=-1` means "use the Minecraft server port."
3. Restart the server.

### If you see "Address already in use" / "Failed to run voice chat at UDP port …"

That means something else on your server is already using that port's UDP — **almost always the Minecraft Query service**, which binds UDP on the same port when it's enabled. Fix it one of two ways in `server.properties`:

- `enable-query=false` (turn Query off if you don't need it — most servers don't), **or**
- `query.port=<some other free port>` (keep Query but move it off the voice port).

Then restart. Voice chat now has the port to itself.

## Option B — a dedicated voice port

If you'd rather keep voice on its own port (and keep Query where it is), your server needs a **second port** allocated. Open a support ticket and we'll add one to your server; then set `port=<that port>` (a real number, not -1) in `voicechat-server.properties` and restart.

## The "Failed to bind to address" warning is harmless

A line like `Failed to bind to address '203.0.113.x', binding to wildcard IP instead` is **normal** on hosted servers — your server doesn't own the raw public IP, so voice chat correctly falls back to binding all interfaces (`0.0.0.0`). Setting `bind_address=0.0.0.0` silences it. **Don't** set `bind_address` to the public IP.

## Checklist

- `port=-1` (or your dedicated port) in `voicechat-server.properties`.
- Query disabled, or on a different port than voice.
- Restart, then confirm the log reads `Voice chat server started at …:<your server port>` with no bind error.
- Players just connect to your server normally — voice works automatically once the port is right.
